title: TBM 415: Demand Mix, Discovery, and AI as a (Dys)function Multiplier
questions: What is the funnel in my organization actually optimizing for, and how was it designed?
Where does our demand come from, how does it form, and how much agency do teams have to shape it?
How can we tell whether our work is producing learning or just negotiation/performance?
If AI accelerates idea generation and execution, what would improve—and what might quietly get worse?
summary: The post argues that product teams struggle not because of generic process gaps, but because organizations rarely analyze the demand mix and the day-to-day activities used to shape and filter incoming work. It introduces a demand-funnel framing (including mandate levels and intake patterns) to explain why “good” practices vary by context, and then examines how AI changes outcomes depending on whether the organization treats engineering as the bottleneck or the system as a learning engine.

title: TBM 400: Strategic Portfolio Management Meets Product-Centricity
questions: What is the true unit of allocation in our organization: projects, flow, teams, or long-lived systems?
When we stop an initiative, what capacity is actually freed versus permanently entangled?
How reversible are our strategic decisions in practice, not just in theory?
Who really owns portfolio decisions—PMO/portfolio function or the executive team—and what levers do they control?
search_terms: liquidity and fungibility in tech strategy
unit of allocation projects vs teams vs systems
summary: The post argues that strategic portfolio management metaphors imported from finance often fail in software and technology because products and teams are low-liquidity, low-fungibility socio-technical systems. It contrasts centralized IT and lean portfolio management assumptions with product-centric leadership, concluding that portfolio decisions must be treated as organizational design and executive stewardship rather than a separate prioritization layer. It’s for leaders and practitioners rethinking how to allocate limited capacity and make strategic bets in product organizations.

title: TBM 394: Imagine If a Restaurant Behaved Like Your Average Product Team (Extended Edition)
questions: How do we prevent our team from mistaking activity for impact?
What would a faster, more visible customer feedback loop look like in product development?
How do leadership incentives create performative productivity and quality decay?
What strategic constraints (a clear menu) stop backlog and scope from turning into menu creep?
summary: The post uses a restaurant-as-product-team metaphor to diagnose why software teams can be exhausted and “at capacity” yet still deliver late, wrong, or low-quality outcomes. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders and practitioners who want to strengthen customer feedback loops, align incentives, and replace performative activity with consistent value delivery.

title: TBM 364: Three Team Vibes
questions: Which team operating mode are we currently in, and what signals show it?
Are we making progress effectively, or just pushing work forward without alignment?
How do dependencies and roadmap ambiguity create decision drift and loss of control?
What cultural incentives (project ownership, productivity metrics, performance reviews) are driving our team mode?
search_terms: team alignment outcome driven vs ambiguous
project-based culture each engineer has a project
summary: This post describes three recurring team “vibes” or operating modes—aligned and outcome-driven, parallel tracks with local progress, and strategically unsettled dependency-tangled—and how each feels in day-to-day execution. It’s for product development leaders and PM/engineering managers who want a stigma-free way to diagnose team dynamics, interpret progress signals, and choose interventions based on context rather than individual blame.

title: TBM 363: Is Matt A Loser?
questions: Is Matt a “loser” or low-agency person, or is he a normal professional navigating a constrained system?
Why doesn’t Matt simply look for another job given the signals?
Is Matt’s anger helping or hurting his ability to create impact?
Should Matt accept the limits and focus on what he controls, or try to enter product operations/product leadership?
search_terms: how to navigate misaligned leadership incentives
why stay at a company that wastes time
summary: The post explores how organizational effectiveness can be blocked by incentive misalignment and power dynamics, using a friend’s experience as a case study. It argues that executives can actively shape narratives and budgets in ways that neutralize effective advice, leading to scapegoating and strategic drift. It’s aimed at leaders and operators trying to understand agency, motivation, and where to focus when adopting a product operating model or similar organizational redesign.

title: TBM 359: Process Person
questions: How do you design a process that works across different contexts and teams?
When should you use structured discovery versus letting teams freestyle?
What constraints (staffing, schedules, tooling) force process, and how do you remove them later?
How can leaders avoid turning process into slide-driven performative status work?
search_terms: quarterly planning waste NPS less than zero
summary: The post argues that process design is inevitable in product organizations, but its quality depends on context, trade-offs, and whether constraints and incentives are addressed. It’s aimed at product, UX, and operations leaders who design workflows and discovery rituals and want to avoid turning them into forms, slide theater, or unnecessary overhead. It also suggests a learning path where structured motions can be used initially, then intentionally loosened as teams mature.

title: TBM 333: Lean Graph Theory
questions: When does Lean thinking help product organizations, and when does it miss the mark?
How should we model an organization when work is both sequential and highly interdependent?
Why do companies chase local maxima when applying Lean to complex project delivery?
What changes when a product org moves from independent teams to deep cross-team collaboration?
summary: The post argues that Lean principles (including WIP limits, continuous improvement, and respect for people) should be applied differently depending on the organization’s context and dominant structure. It proposes modeling product organizations as overlapping graph types—path (sequences), DAG (dependencies/approvals), and networks (feedback, collaboration, ecosystems)—and suggests that as cross-team interdependence grows, organizations should shift emphasis toward network and DAG thinking. It is aimed at leaders and practitioners designing product operating systems who are trying to decide how to stabilize execution without over-centralizing or optimizing locally.

title: The "X" Files (65.57% of 2024 Edition)
questions: How do we compare progress across teams without assuming apples-to-apples contexts?
What’s the best way to set delivery dates—fixed date variable scope or confidence ranges—and how do we communicate uncertainty?
How can leaders reduce WIP, dependency churn, and administrative overhead that functions like debt?
What metrics can actually measure developer performance and team impact instead of encouraging gaming?
search_terms: agile sprint standardization apples to apples
fixed date variable scope software delivery
construct validity content validity developer metrics
blame culture complainers and problem solving
summary: This post uses a recurring dialogue format to explore common leadership and product-management tensions in software organizations, especially around agile planning, prioritization, and accountability. It’s aimed at product, engineering, and leadership practitioners who want sharper ways to talk about delivery uncertainty, reduce organizational “debt,” and avoid misleading performance metrics. The underlying message is that many “process” demands are symptoms of incentive and org-design problems rather than solvable by more standardization alone.

title: TBM 297: Staying In Touch
questions: How can a team avoid forgetting what changed over the last year?
What lightweight tracking practices create useful baselines for diagnosing shifts in lead time and effort?
How do narrative drift and human memory limitations distort a team’s understanding of past work?
What retro activities or documentation methods help teams preserve context for future retrospectives?
search_terms: reference feature types lead time tracking
summary: The post connects personal fitness tracking with organizational learning, arguing that both individuals and teams lose context over time and struggle to diagnose change without durable reference points. It offers practical practices for product development teams—lightweight categorization with lead-time tracking, reviewing past retro notes, and creating “time capsules” to preserve current perspective—aimed at reducing organizational forgetting. The intended audience is engineering and product leaders who run retrospectives and want better ways to understand how their team’s context evolves.

title: TBM 293: Where More Effective Product Teams Spend More (and Less) Time
questions: Where should a product team spend more time and energy to improve effectiveness?
Which activities are draining a team’s focus without directly creating customer value?
How can teams make decisions that last long enough to drive action?
What signals show whether a team is truly outcome-oriented versus output-oriented?
search_terms: dependency management and project management overhead
summary: The post argues that product team effectiveness can be diagnosed by examining where teams spend their limited time and energy. It contrasts high-performing behaviors—customer research, data synthesis, equal-footing collaboration, and durable decision-making—with common low-value drains like estimation theater, internal request triage, unusable data, excessive planning, and reactive work. It’s aimed at product leaders and PMs who want practical guidance to shift team calendars toward customer value.

title: TBM 277: Bring Back Fun
questions: How do we rekindle fun and joy in product teams when everything feels serious and efficiency-focused?
What causes workplace division and low loyalty in tech organizations, and how does it become self-reinforcing?
Should productivity and efficiency be prioritized over the human experience of building impactful things?
How can leaders create conditions where teams celebrate wins and feel safe to be honest?
search_terms: how to improve team morale in product management
summary: The post argues that product makers and tech teams thrive when they can enjoy challenging, meaningful work with interesting people and minimal “bullshit.” It connects current workplace seriousness, divisive narratives, and job-market anxiety to a self-reinforcing doom loop that erodes fun and loyalty. It’s aimed at product leaders and builders who want practical cultural direction for restoring joy, celebration, and impact in teams.

title: TBM 276: Leverage Distribution
questions: How should we think about team productivity beyond simple efficiency metrics?
What does it mean to allocate effort toward high-leverage work, and how can we measure it?
Why do small process or decision-quality issues compound into big team performance differences?
How do negative ROI activities (technical debt, dependencies, misguided requests) affect morale and long-term outcomes?
search_terms: high leverage work vs low leverage work
negative ROI work and team morale
summary: The post proposes a mental model for team effectiveness based on how teams distribute time and energy across high-leverage (high ROI) versus negative-ROI work. It argues that small allocation differences can create outsized long-term impact through compounding virtuous loops or exponential headwinds. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners trying to improve team outcomes beyond surface-level productivity and efficiency discussions.

title: TBM 259: The Predictability Trap
questions: Should predictable delivery be the goal or a supporting metric for value creation?
How do you achieve predictable innovation without turning delivery into commitment theater?
What operational levers actually improve delivery predictability beyond better estimates?
What happens when a company demands predictability while teams carry accumulated debt and cannot slow down?
search_terms: lean flow levers WIP batch size
summary: The post argues that “predictable delivery” should serve value creation and sustainable differentiated growth, not replace them as the primary objective. It contrasts businesses that depend on predictable feature delivery (and therefore obsess over commitments) with teams that need predictable innovation by managing complexity and trimming low-value work. It also claims that delivery predictability is driven more by flow and execution practices than by better estimates, and that demanding predictability while carrying accumulated debt can eventually make it unattainable.

title: TBM 247: 15 Metaphors for Waste In Product Development
questions: What does product development waste look like when it’s visible?
How can teams detect invisible waste early, before it becomes debt or backlog?
When should we add slack instead of pushing utilization higher?
How do incentives and organizational design cause teams to produce activity without outcomes?
summary: The post uses 15 metaphors to make “invisible waste” in product development feel concrete, helping readers recognize patterns where teams create activity without value. It’s aimed at product leaders, engineers, and operators who want practical mental models for diagnosing waste caused by debt, over-constraint, utilization pressure, misaligned incentives, and organizational bottlenecks.

title: TBM 240: The Ultimate Guide to Developer Counter-Productivity
questions: Where does my team’s developer time and energy actually go each day?
Which recurring activities are creating the most low or negative leverage work for developers?
How can managers reallocate time away from reactive work, meetings, and overhead toward higher-leverage engineering work?
Why do the same developers become more or less productive in different environments?
summary: The post argues that developer productivity is less about individual traits and more about the organizational environment that forces developers into predictable low/negative leverage work. It provides a categorized checklist of where developers spend time and energy—such as reactive firefighting, context switching, administrative/compliance drag, ineffective meetings, and dependency overhead—then suggests a two-week tracking exercise for teams and a reallocation exercise for managers. It’s aimed at engineering leaders and teams who want to improve performance by reducing systemic waste rather than measuring individuals.

title: TBM 237: Incompetence!
questions: How can two teams in the same company show radically different time allocation without obvious incompetence?
What causes a team to drift from effective performance to an ineffective state over time?
How should leaders interpret time-tracking data when context and incentives differ?
What enables a team to shift from a “Team 2” environment to a healthier “Team 1” state?
summary: The post uses a time-allocation tracking experiment to challenge the instinct to explain large productivity differences as incompetence. It argues that team performance is shaped by long-term context, organizational inertia, and gradual cultural shifts, and that leaders should distrust time estimates unless people feel safe to report accurately. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders trying to interpret metrics and drive healthier team transitions.

title: TBM 236: The Paper-Cut Tax
questions: How do we measure and communicate the opportunity cost of constant low-level friction in product work?
Why is waste in knowledge work harder to justify than downtime in operations?
How can teams discuss recurring problems without triggering blame or defensiveness?
What practices help reduce the paper-cut tax over time?
summary: The post argues that product teams can suffer a “paper-cut tax” from frequent small frictions that consume time and leverage without clear downtime. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders who need ways to explain, quantify, and reduce these issues despite a quantification gap, workaround normalization, and blame concerns. It offers practical interventions like immediate logging, stigma reduction, valuing subjective context, and rough opportunity-cost estimates.

title: TBM 235: Forms & Shadows
questions: Why do some product teams feel coherent while others constantly shift priorities and translate between contexts?
How does organizational structure interfere with implementing value-and-customer-first ways of working?
What causes decision drift and “why are we doing this again?” energy in product teams?
What interventions can reduce cognitive load without locking the organization into rigid processes?
search_terms: cognitive load from context switching teams
summary: The post argues that product teams either thrive or struggle depending on whether key organizational “frames” (strategy, structure, customers, incentives, goals, releases, knowledge, etc.) are coherent with each other. It’s aimed at product leaders and practitioners who want to diagnose symptoms like context switching, decision drift, and misaligned priorities, and who are considering interventions such as temporary scaffolding and attractors to restore alignment.

title: TBM 234: Maintenance, KTLO, and BAU
questions: How can we categorize “maintenance/KTLO” work without marginalizing high-leverage contributions?
What model can replace discrete KTLO vs innovation buckets with a continuum of work types?
How do accounting and capitalization incentives distort team behavior toward flashy projects?
How can we reduce low-leverage “keeping the lights on” work by increasing proactive, multiplicative leverage?
search_terms: keeping the lights on vs innovation
summary: This post argues that “maintenance,” “keeping the lights on” (KTLO), and “business as usual” are often stigmatized despite being potentially high-leverage work in software teams. It critiques discrete bucket planning and accounting-driven incentives that can reward big-bang projects over many small compounding improvements, and proposes a continuum-based model to classify work by reactivity, leverage, transformation vs preservation, and direct vs indirect impact.

title: TBM 231: Productivity (The Clash)
questions: Why do productivity discussions in my company feel like culture or power fights rather than problem-solving?
How do leadership and product teams end up with incompatible productivity paradigms?
What changes when organizations shift from boom-time to war-time conditions?
How can we address productivity disagreements without escalating blame and reactivity?
search_terms: organizational habits boom times lean times
summary: The post argues that productivity debates are frequently proxy battles over organizational meaning, power, and worldview, intensified by threat during downturns. It’s aimed at leaders and product/ops practitioners who are trying to interpret “productivity” disagreements and decide whether to engage rationally or focus on listening and bridging cultural paradigms.

title: TBM 228: What Slows Us Down
questions: Why do companies that value speed still end up moving slowly?
What causes teams to keep shipping quickly until things start to drag?
Which mental models of product development lead to bloat and accumulated debt?
How can a business model enable sustainable velocity rather than temporary momentum?
summary: The post argues that organizational speed is constrained by incentives and psychology, especially survival pressure and success-driven inertia that can turn into gluttony. It is aimed at product and engineering leaders who want to diagnose why teams slow down over time and how flawed development metaphors undermine sustainable velocity. The author emphasizes that aligning business model, quality, customer value, and iterative collaboration is key, and that mental models typically change only through lived experience.

title: TBM 201: The Myth of Quality Tradeoffs
questions: Is quality really something you have to trade off against speed and innovation?
Why do some organizations keep quality relatively high under the same delivery pressures?
How can teams ship fast while still improving quality over time?
What happens when an organization never explicitly defines quality?
search_terms: accidental quality drift vs deliberate tradeoffs
summary: This post challenges the common narrative that teams must balance quality against speed by reframing many “tradeoff” stories as post-hoc rationalizations for quality drift under uncertainty and short-term pressure. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders and practitioners who want ways to achieve innovation and fast delivery without sacrificing quality, emphasizing shared quality definitions, patience, skills, organizational design, and leadership beliefs.

title: TBM 5/52: Three Product Teams
questions: Which product team model best fits our environment and constraints?
How should we balance upstream exploration with downstream delivery?
What beliefs are driving our role boundaries between designers, developers, and product managers?
How do we choose between sustainable sprint commitments and mission-based team motion?
summary: The post compares three popular product team operating models and the role-based beliefs that typically accompany each: sprint/story/backlog-centric, team-and-mission-centric, and engineer-centric with “rockstar” PMs. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to diagnose how their assumptions about designers, developers, PMs, and work flow shape outcomes, collaboration, and customer proximity. The takeaway is that no model is universally best; teams should evaluate their own beliefs and context to choose and adapt practices.

title: TBM 14/52: Less Mono-Process (VIDEO)
questions: How do teams end up using the same process for every type of work?
What are the risks of a mono-process approach?
How can teams choose different processes for different work types?
search_terms: one process for all work problems
summary: The post highlights how teams can fall into the “mono-process” trap—using one standard way of working for all types of tasks. It’s aimed at team leads and practitioners who want to improve delivery by tailoring processes to the nature of the work.

title: TBM 10/52: What Didn’t End Up in Jira
questions: Why doesn’t Jira (or similar tools) reflect what really happened on a project?
How can we reconstruct project history when our tracking system is incomplete?
What parts of work are systematically missing from our tooling (people, decisions, context)?
How do we use retrospective evidence to improve future estimates?
search_terms: what didn't end up in Jira
summary: The post highlights a common mismatch between what teams do and what their work-tracking tools (like Jira) record, using a small initiative as an example. It’s aimed at teams and leaders who want to understand why estimates and tool data diverge, and encourages reconstructing past work history to calibrate expectations and improve learning.

title: TBM 1/52: Rough Mixes
questions: How can teams use integration points to drive creativity instead of process compliance?
What does “making something that works” look like in an agile sprint?
How do feedback and repeated demo iterations help converge on a finished product?
What real-world test methods can replace purely internal review cycles?
search_terms: music production metaphor for product teams
summary: The post uses teenage music “rough mixes” and car-based listening rituals as a metaphor for how teams should build products: integrate early, test in the real world, and iterate via feedback. It argues that sprint frameworks often miss this purpose by overemphasizing planning mechanics instead of the integration forcing function that drives improvement. It’s aimed at product and engineering teams looking to make agile execution more creative and outcome-focused.

title: TBM 49/52: Your Calendar = Your Priorities
questions: How can I tell what my team truly values by looking at our calendars?
What counts as 'valuable' work in knowledge work when it’s not always efficient or customer-facing?
Why do teams keep filling time with meetings and busywork instead of the most important work?
What practices help teams unblock work and sustain aligned autonomy without micromanaging?
summary: This post argues that calendars are a practical diagnostic for uncovering what individuals and teams actually prioritize in knowledge work. It helps leaders and teams redefine “valuable” beyond efficiency, certainty, or customer-facing activity, and use a lightweight team audit to spot non-value-add patterns like meeting churn and heroics. It then outlines organizational practices—transparency, aligned autonomy, work visibility, and unblocking system drag—that enable teams to spend time on the most valuable work.

title: TBM 31/52: The Weeds
questions: When we say “get out of the weeds,” which work are we actually removing or shifting?
Are we empowering teams to own creative work and definition, or just pushing project management overhead elsewhere?
What is the source of our process overhead, and which role should carry it?
How should we balance strategy time with necessary craft and discovery work over time?
search_terms: free up product managers strategy vs execution
role boundaries product team engineering manager project management
collectivism vs specialization in org design
summary: This post reframes “the weeds” as three different kinds of work—creative process, definition/specification, and project management—showing that different teams use the phrase with opposite meanings. It’s for product leaders and engineering leaders considering org/role shifts, arguing that “freeing up” product managers can either distribute responsibility well or simply move overhead onto engineering managers. The post emphasizes spreading product/design/technology thinking across multiple “hats” and managing the natural ebb and flow between strategy and craft.

title: TBM 26/52: Product Team Health (and Performance) "Signals"
questions: How can you tell whether product teams are healthy or high-performing quickly?
What signals should you listen for in the first 15–20 minutes of a conversation with a team?
Why is 'maturity' a misleading concept for evaluating product teams?
How do decision authority, dependencies, and learning practices affect team performance?
search_terms: how to assess product team maturity
summary: The post argues that you can’t reliably judge product team health or performance by asking about “maturity” or assuming a single cause. Instead, it offers a set of observable signals—like flow, structured learning, higher decision authority, real product strategy, experimentation/impact framing, fewer dependencies, and direct customer understanding—aimed at leaders and product practitioners who need fast, practical ways to assess team effectiveness.

title: TBM 23/52: The Mediocrity Trap
questions: How can we recognize when we’re stuck in mediocre work rather than learning from a miss?
What causes teams to escalate commitment and keep investing in mediocre outcomes?
How do we reduce WIP and limit big projects in progress to avoid mediocrity?
What practices help an organization define and measure “awesome” instead of relying on success theater?
search_terms: escalation of commitment in product teams
sunk cost fallacy in software/product work
big projects in progress (BPIP) mediocrity probability
define awesome success stories and postmortems
summary: This post argues that mediocre work is a high-cost organizational failure mode that drains morale and hides behind success theater. It’s aimed at product and engineering teams who want practical ways to prevent escalation of commitment, limit WIP, and build a culture that defines “awesome” and publicizes misses so learning leads to better decisions.

title: TBM 52/53: Real Teams (Not Groups of One-Person Teams)
questions: How do we know whether our “teams” are actually teams or just groups of one-person projects?
What incentives and org structures cause engineers to prefer individual projects over collaboration?
How does one-person team design affect WIP, knowledge sharing, and cross-training?
What practical steps can reduce “Tetris playing” and increase team ownership without forcing pairing or mobbing?
search_terms: teams vs groups of one-person projects
summary: The post argues that many organizations label collections of individuals as “teams,” but operate them as one-person project groups managed through individual backlogs. It explains why this pattern persists (easier management, less confrontation, short-term throughput) and the downstream harms (silos, high WIP, weaker collaboration, and reduced resilience). It is aimed at engineering leaders and product development organizations trying to build real, fluid product teams rather than backlog-managed individuals.

title: TBM 43/53: The Product Outcomes Formula®™©
questions: Where is my product team currently bottlenecked across data, insights, and action?
How do we balance volume and quality in our data and decision-making?
What happens to the data-to-action loop when product teams scale into specialized silos?
How can psychological safety and cross-functional agency improve insight and action quality?
search_terms: data usability insights rate action quality
data to insights to action loop
qualitative data literacy for product teams
summary: The post proposes a simple model for thinking about how teams “use data” to drive product outcomes, framing the work as a loop from customer data to insights to shipped experiments. It’s aimed at product teams and leaders who want to diagnose bottlenecks by evaluating six levers (data usability/volume, insight rate/quality, action rate/quality) and understand how scaling and organizational silos can disrupt the loop.

title: TBM 30/53: Healthy Forcing Functions (and Paying Attention)
questions: How can we design healthy forcing functions to prevent deep burnout?
Which work practices act as anti-patterns and increase burnout risk during high-stress periods?
Are our current forcing functions helping people stay attentive or pushing them into dissociation and numbness?
How can teams co-design forcing functions that support sustainable flow and recovery?
summary: The post defines forcing functions as design choices that interrupt automatic behavior and force conscious attention, then applies that idea to burnout prevention. It argues that certain planning and delivery anti-patterns (especially long batch commitments without complementary constraints) can worsen burnout, while practices like shorter mission cycles, usability testing blocks, and scheduled recharge help teams stay attentive and maintain sustainable flow. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders and teams looking to co-design work systems that protect people during high-stress periods.

title: TBM 29/53: Shipping Faster Than You Learn (or…)
questions: Are we shipping faster than we learn, or learning faster than we ship?
What is causing our product complexity to clog the flow of work and slow learning?
How do we prevent customer-feedback-driven work from becoming generally good but not transformative?
Which forcing functions and learning practices should we institutionalize to balance shipping and learning?
search_terms: product development feedback loop vs factory line
forcing functions for learning and shipping
weekly usability testing and customer interviews
summary: This post argues that product teams often fall into an imbalance where shipping outpaces learning (or learning piles up due to complexity), leading to frustration and incremental outcomes. It’s aimed at product, design, and product analytics practitioners who want practical ways to visualize the work system as a loop and add forcing functions—like learning reviews, exploratory research, usability testing, and kill-a-feature days—to build both learning and shipping capabilities.

title: TBM 28/53: Developer & Designer Collaboration (in the Real World)
questions: How should developers and designers collaborate when work isn’t predictable?
What does an effort need right now from each teammate?
How do we balance sprint/Agile pressure with designers’ need for deep work?
How can teams adapt their working agreements instead of following “The One Way”?
summary: The post uses a real-world developer-designer collaboration example to argue that effective teamwork often involves alternating individual and collaborative work, with divergence followed by frequent integration. It’s aimed at product and delivery teams (and their managers) who feel pressured to follow rigid Agile/UX “rules,” encouraging them to adapt working agreements to the specific needs of the effort and teammates.
